title: Discovery of Strong Energy-Dependent X-ray Polarization in the Intermediate State of GS 1354-64
summary: We report the discovery of significant X-ray polarization from the dynamically confirmed black hole X-ray binary (BHXB) GS 1354-64 during its 2025-2026 outburst, obtained with the Imaging X-ray Polarimetry Explorer (IXPE). The observation, obtained shortly after a bright X-ray flare, captures the source in an intermediate state following a stalled (failed) state transition. We discover significant 2-8 keV polarization at the ~4% level with high statistical support--14-sigma significance from frequentist analysis and log Bayes Factor 283+/-1 in a Bayesian framework--measuring PD 4.0+/-0.2% and PA-1+/-2 degrees (90% credible interval). The PD exhibits a statistically significant increasing trend with energy--the strongest such increase yet observed by IXPE in a BHXB--going from 2.1+/-0.3% in the 2-3 keV band to 11+/-3% in the 6.5-7 keV band, while the PA appears stable across both energy and time to within statistical uncertainties. Timing analysis of the IXPE data reveals a ~5 Hz Type-C quasi-periodic oscillation. IXPE + NuSTAR spectropolarimetric modeling suggests that the data can be described by polarized thermal disk and Comptonized components with PAs differing by ~90 degrees, or by a dominant Comptonized polarized component whose effective PD increases across the IXPE bandpass--the inferred component-level polarization levels are therefore model-dependent. In either picture, GS 1354-64 retains a strong coronal component during the transitional period observed by IXPE. These results illustrate how X-ray polarimetry can provide a sensitive diagnostic of the accretion state and geometry in black hole X-ray binary accretion flows, exploring a liminal phase at the cusp of state transition.

AI.tldr: The paper presents the discovery of significant X-ray polarization from the black hole X-ray binary GS 1354-64 during its 2025-2026 outburst, observed with IXPE. The polarization degree (PD) increases with energy, and the source retains a strong coronal component during a transitional accretion state.
AI.motivation: To study the accretion geometry and state transitions in black hole X-ray binaries (BHXBs) using X-ray polarimetry, focusing on the transitional phase following a stalled state transition.
AI.method: Analysis of IXPE and NuSTAR data to measure polarization properties (PD and PA) across energy bands, timing analysis to identify quasi-periodic oscillations, and spectropolarimetric modeling to interpret component contributions (thermal disk vs. Comptonized emission).
AI.result: Detected PD of ~4% at 2-8 keV with high significance, showing a strong energy-dependent increase in PD (2.1% at 2-3 keV to 11% at 6.5-7 keV), stable PA, and evidence for either orthogonal polarized components (disk vs. corona) or energy-varying Comptonization. A 5 Hz Type-C QPO was also observed.
AI.conclusion: X-ray polarimetry effectively probes accretion flow geometry and state transitions in BHXBs, highlighting the utility of IXPE in studying transient phases critical for understanding black hole physics.

title: High-energy neutrino emission from the Type~IIn supernova SN~2017hcd
comment: 30 pages, 8 figures, 2 tables. Comments are welcome
summary: Neutrino astronomy provides another window to exploring the Universe, exemplified by the detection of a megaelectronvolt neutrino burst from the core-collapse supernova (CCSN) SN~1987A (refs.~\citenum{hir+87,bio+87}). Commonly discussed theories suggest that some CCSNe could produce neutrinos with energies a thousand times more than those of SN~1987A \cite{tm18}, which has been probed with new-generation facilities \cite{abb+12,aar+15,abb+23}. The interaction of SN ejecta with a dense circumstellar medium (CSM) or a jet, launched in a CCSN, being choked in the stellar envelope of the progenitor or an outside CSM are both well-accepted scenarios for the high-energy neutrino production. Here we report the detection of a high-energy neutrino flare at a 3.9$σ$ significance from SN~2017hcd, made by our analysis of the public track-like neutrino data taken by the IceCube Neutrino Observatory \cite{IceCube17}. A Type IIn SN with optical emissions arising from the ejecta--CSM interaction, SN~2017hcd's neutrino flare lasted $\sim$1--2 month, with its central time $\sim$14-day prior to the SN's optical discovery time. Its estimated isotropic neutrino energy (all flavors) is approximately two orders of magnitude higher than the energy ($\sim 10^{50}$\,erg) carried in the SN's ejecta, too high to be explained with the ejecta--CSM scenario. Thus, a choked jet may be the source of the neutrino flare.

AI.tldr: The paper reports a high-energy neutrino flare detected from SN 2017hcd at 3.9σ significance, suggesting a choked jet origin since the ejecta-CSM interaction cannot explain the observed energy.
AI.motivation: To explore supernova neutrino emission mechanisms and test theories predicting higher-energy neutrinos than SN 1987A, using new observational data from IceCube.
AI.method: Analysis of IceCube neutrino Observatory's public track-like data to identify a neutrino flare temporally associated with SN 2017hcd's ejecta-CSM interaction, comparing energy estimates with theoretical models.
AI.result: Detection of a ~1-2 month duration neutrino flare ~14 days before optical discovery, with isotropic energy ~10^52 erg - two orders of magnitude higher than ejecta energy, incompatible with ejecta-CSM scenario.
AI.conclusion: The observed neutrino flare likely originates from a choked jet in the progenitor star, providing evidence for choked jets in type IIn supernovae and a new neutrino production mechanism.

title: Evolution of Time-Lags of Swift J1727.8-1613 during the Rising Phase of Its Discovery Outburst
comment: 10 pages, 4 figures, 1 table, Accepted for publication in MNRAS
summary: We investigate the accretion dynamics of the black hole X-ray binary Swift J1727.8-1613 during its $2023-2024$ discovery outburst that lasted for $\sim10$ months. Insight-HXMT monitored the rising phase of the outburst of Swift J1727.8-1613 roughly continuously from 2023 Aug 25 to 2023 Oct 05. Strong signatures of type-C Quasi-Periodic Oscillations (QPOs) are observed during this phase of the outburst. In our recent paper, nature of the QPOs are studied with the propagating oscillatory shock (POS) model. In this paper, we report on the observation of both positive (or hard) and negative (or soft) time-lags in the $4-10$ keV (LE), $10-30$ keV (ME), and $30 -150$ keV (HE) bands, computed with respect to the $2-4$ keV reference band. We detect a clear transition from hard to soft lags as the outburst evolves. We show the evolution of QPOs and associated time-lags between different X-ray energy bands, correlated with changes in the QPO frequency, spectral state, and the size of the Comptonizing region. Our analysis reveals strong anti-correlations between the time-lags and both QPO frequency and photon index, and a strong positive correlation with the shock location. These evolving lag characteristics and their correlations provide crucial insights into the changing accretion geometry and the interplay of radiative processes, further supporting dynamic models like the POS in explaining the coupled spectro-temporal evolution in black hole X-ray binaries.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes accretion dynamics of the black hole X-ray binary Swift J1727.8-1613 during its 2023-2024 outburst using Insight-HXMT data, focusing on type-C QPOs and time-lags across energy bands. Detected lag transitions from hard to soft, and correlations between lags, QPO frequency, spectral state, and shock location support the POS model's role in explaining accretion geometry changes.
AI.motivation: To understand the accretion mechanisms in black hole X-ray binaries (BHXRBs) by studying the temporal and spectral evolution during outbursts, particularly using QPOs and time-lags as diagnostics of accretion geometry and radiative processes.
AI.method: Continuous monitoring of Swift J1727.8-1613's 2023-2024 outburst rise phase with Insight-HXMT. Time-lag analysis between energy bands (4-150 keV) using a 2-4 keV reference. Correlated QPO properties (frequency), spectral parameters (photon index, Comptonizing region size), and shock location from the POS model with time-lag evolution.
AI.result: Observed hard-to-soft lag transitions over the outburst duration. Found strong anti-correlations between time-lags and QPO frequency/photon index, and positive correlation with shock location. These support dynamic models like POS, linking lags to accretion geometry changes and radiative processes.
AI.conclusion: The evolutionary lag characteristics and observed correlations validate the POS model's applicability in explaining BHXRB spectro-temporal dynamics. The study emphasizes time-lags as key probes of accretion disk structure and shock dynamics during outbursts.

title: Regular Bardeen Black Hole Solutions in Rastall Theory: A Gravitational Decoupling Approach
summary: This research applies the generalized technique of gravitational decoupling to the Bardeen black hole, producing novel black hole solutions in the context of Rastall theory. We proceed by decomposition of the field equations corresponding to an additional matter source into two sets, for further considerations. The metric functions of the Bardeen black hole are adopted to specify the first set. The second one, which is subject to an extra source, is resolved considering a linear equation of state of matter. Through the integration of the solutions of these sets, we develop two expanded models and conduct an in-depth analysis of their distinct physical characteristics, governed by specific parameters. We investigate thermodynamic quantities like density, anisotropic pressure, energy bounds, asymptotic flatness, and thermodynamical properties like the Hawking temperature, entropy, and specific heat, etc. Both models are asymptotically flat but violate the energy bounds. Furthermore, the density, radial pressure, and Hawking temperature demonstrate consistent and acceptable behavior. Ultimately, the thermodynamic stability is affirmed through the analysis of specific heat and the Hessian matrix.

AI.tldr: The paper applies gravitational decoupling to Bardeen black holes in Rastall theory, creating new solutions with analysis of their physical and thermodynamic properties.
AI.motivation: To explore novel black hole solutions using gravitational decoupling in Rastall theory and examine their validity through thermodynamic and physical characteristic analysis.
AI.method: Decomposing field equations into two sets using the Bardeen metric functions and a linear equation of state, then integrating solutions to form two models analyzed for parameters like energy bounds, thermodynamics, and stability.
AI.result: Two asymptotically flat models were developed, though they violate energy bounds. Key properties like density and temperature showed acceptable behavior, and thermodynamic stability was confirmed via specific heat and Hessian matrix analysis.
AI.conclusion: The models successfully extend Bardeen black hole solutions in Rastall theory, offering insights despite energy bound violations, with validated thermodynamic stability through specific metrics.

title: A Short-Timescale Optical Quasi-Periodic Oscillation in PKS\,0805$-$07 from High-Cadence TESS Observations
summary: We present a timing analysis of the high-cadence optical light curve of the high-redshift flat-spectrum radio quasar PKS\,0805$-$07 obtained during \textit{TESS} Sector~34 (MJD $59230.90$--$59239.90$). We search for short-timescale quasi-periodic oscillations (QPOs) using complementary time-series techniques, including the Lomb--Scargle periodogram (LSP) and the weighted wavelet $Z$-transform (WWZ), and evaluate their significance against red-noise variability using Monte Carlo simulations. The LSP reveals a dominant modulation at $f \approx 0.597\,\mathrm{d^{-1}}$ ($P \approx 1.7\,\mathrm{d}$) exceeding the $99.99\%$ confidence level, while the WWZ independently recovers a consistent timescale at the $\sim 99.9\%$ level and shows that the signal is temporally localized rather than persistent across the full light curve. The modulation spans $\sim 5$ coherent cycles, indicating a transient quasi-periodic feature. We discuss possible physical interpretations of the detected modulation. In a disk-based scenario, orbital motion of a hotspot near the innermost stable circular orbit implies a black hole mass of $M_{\rm BH}\sim7.2\times10^{8}\,M_\odot$, consistent with typical FSRQ values. Alternatively, magnetohydrodynamic kink instabilities in the relativistic jet can naturally produce day-scale variability for standard blazar parameters and account for the transient character of the signal. We conclude that the observed modulation is consistent with a compact, short-lived structure embedded within stochastic jet variability.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a timing analysis of the optical light curve of the quasar PKS 0805-07 using TESS data, revealing a short-timescale quasi-periodic oscillation (QPO) at ~1.7 days. The analysis employs Lomb-Scargle periodogram and WWZ methods, confirming the transient nature of the QPO. Possible explanations include a hotspot near the black hole's inner orbit or jet instabilities, with the latter being more consistent with the transient signal.
AI.motivation: To investigate short-timescale quasi-periodic oscillations in the light curve of a high-redshift blazar to understand the physical mechanisms driving its variability and constrain black hole parameters.
AI.method: Time-series analysis using Lomb-Scargle periodogram and weighted wavelet Z-transform on TESS optical data. Monte Carlo simulations assessed red-noise significance. Physical interpretations considered orbital hotspot motion and magnetohydrodynamic jet instabilities.
AI.result: A dominant QPO at ~1.7 days was detected exceeding 99.99% confidence in LSP and ~99.9% in WWZ, lasting ~5 cycles. The signal is localized in time, not persistent. The black hole mass derived from orbital models matches typical FSRQ values. Jet instabilities explain the transient nature better.
AI.conclusion: The modulation likely originates from a compact, short-lived jet structure, supporting either a transient hotspot or jet kink instability mechanism, with the latter aligning better with observed variability characteristics.

title: Dark Energy from Entanglements with Mirror Universe
summary: We investigate a possible resolution of the dark energy problem within a pair-universe framework, in which the Universe emerges as an entangled pair of time-reversed sectors. In this setting, a global zero-energy condition allows vacuum energy contributions from the two sectors to cancel, alleviating the need for extreme fine-tuning. We propose that the observed dark energy does not originate from vacuum fluctuations but instead arises as an effective entanglement energy between the visible universe and its mirror counterpart. Treating the cosmological constant as an integration constant fixed by boundary conditions rather than a fundamental parameter, we show that the cosmological equations can be formulated without explicitly introducing vacuum energy. By imposing physically motivated boundary conditions at the cosmological event horizon, we obtain an integration constant consistent with the observed dark energy density. The parallel mirror world scenario thus provides a unified framework that may simultaneously explain the origins of dark energy and dark matter.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a pair-universe framework with entangled, time-reversed sectors to resolve the dark energy problem by canceling vacuum energy contributions and interpreting dark energy as entanglement energy between universes. It also connects this to dark matter explanation.
AI.motivation: To address the extreme fine-tuning problem of the cosmological constant and explain dark energy's origin without relying on vacuum fluctuations.
AI.method: Proposes a pair-universe model with entangled, time-reversed sectors under a global zero-energy condition. Treats the cosmological constant as an integration constant determined by boundary conditions at the cosmological event horizon.
AI.result: Derives an integration constant matching observed dark energy density, unifying dark energy and dark matter explanations within a mirror universe framework.
AI.conclusion: The pair-universe model offers a framework where dark energy arises as entanglement energy between sectors, eliminating the need for fine-tuning and potentially explaining both dark energy and dark matter.

title: Higgs Boson Production in Association with a Single Top Quark as a Probe of the Top Yukawa Coupling
comment: 24 pages, 9 figures, submitted for presentation at the XXIV CONFERENCE ON HIGH ENERGY PHYSICS AND NUCLEAR PHYSICS (Kharkiv, 2026)
summary: This paper provides a detailed analysis of the associated production of the Higgs boson with a single top quark ($tH$) in proton-proton collisions at $\sqrt{s} = 13~\mathrm{TeV}$ and $14~\mathrm{TeV}$. Based on the ATLAS search, we have employed innovative modeling approaches to improve the sensitivity to new physics and improve Standard Model constraints. The major goals are to optimize the data selection, statistical error estimation, determination of physical limits on the top quark Yukawa coupling ($κ_t$), and future experimental projections for HL-LHC. Simulations with MadGraph5aMC@NLO at LO+MLM give cross-sections of $σ_{tHq}$ and $σ_{tWh}$ in SM, scaled by K-factors to simulate NLO accuracy. For inverted $κ_t = -1$ (ITC), positive enhancements are seen, consistent with constructive interference. Kinematic distributions ($H_T$, $p_T(h)$, $η[j]$, $ΔR[t,h]$) are checked against ATLAS expectations, confirming the optimized approach to maximize signal extraction and suppress systematics.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an analysis of $tH$ production in proton-proton collisions at 13 and 14 TeV using ATLAS data, introducing new modeling techniques to enhance sensitivity to new physics and constrain the Standard Model. Key contributions include optimized data selection, statistical improvements, and projections for the HL-LHC.
AI.motivation: To improve constraints on the Standard Model top quark Yukawa coupling ($κ_t$) and explore potential new physics by enhancing the sensitivity of the $tH$ production analysis in ATLAS data.
AI.method: Uses MadGraph5aMC@NLO simulations at LO+MLM to compute cross-sections for $σ_{tHq}$ and $σ_{tWh}$, scaled with K-factors for NLO accuracy. Implements optimized data selection, statistical error estimation, and checks kinematic distributions against ATLAS expectations.
AI.result: Observed constructive interference effects in inverted $κ_t = -1$ scenarios, validated through kinematic distributions matching ATLAS data. Demonstrated effectiveness of optimized methods in maximizing signal extraction and reducing systematic uncertainties.
AI.conclusion: The proposed methods improve precision in $tH$ analysis, enabling better limits on $κ_t$ and providing valuable projections for the High-Luminosity LHC (HL-LHC).

title: Updating the Sensitivity Curves of the STIS Echelles (Post-SM4)
summary: The STIS team re-derived on-orbit sensitivity curves for the echelle modes for post-servicing mission 4 observations using the standard DA white dwarf G 191-B2B. These new updates relied on the recent CALSPECv11 models, which introduced improvements in the fluxes of the primary standard stars of the order of~1-3% depending on the wavelength of interest. As part of this effort, the team also released new blaze shift coefficients and echelle ripple tables. We present a detailed description of the procedures followed in the derivation of these new throughputs and the accompanying updates.

AI.tldr: The STIS team updated sensitivity curves and related parameters for post-Servicing Mission 4 observations using improved CALSPECv11 models and G 191-B2B white dwarf data, achieving ~1-3% flux improvements and releasing new calibration tools.
AI.motivation: To enhance observational accuracy by updating calibration parameters using the latest models and standards.
AI.method: Re-derived sensitivity curves using G 191-B2B data with CALSPECv11, updated blaze shift coefficients, and echelle ripple tables.
AI.result: Achieved 1-3% flux improvements in sensitivity curves, released new calibration coefficients and tables.
AI.conclusion: The updates provide more accurate calibrations for STIS echelle mode observations post-Servicing Mission 4.

title: Global Structure of Accretion Flows in Sgr A*
summary: Sagittarius A* (Sgr A*) is a compact radio source at the Galactic center. Observations have confirmed that its mass is approximately (4.1)*10$^{6}$ M$_{\odot}$, and Sgr A* is generally believed to be powered by gas accretion onto a supermassive black hole. Multifrequency radio observations of the pulsar J1745-2900, about 0.12 pc away from Sgr A*, reveal an unusually large Faraday rotation. Combined with X-ray observations, this indicates that there is a strong magnetic field (greater than 8 mG) leading to a low $β$ plasma at large scales.We show that the gas starts to be captured by the black hole below tens of thousands of the Schwarzschild radii $r_S$, where the gas pressure starts to dominate. Assuming that the accretion rate along magnetic fields at large scales decreases with the distance to the black hole following a power law, it is shown that, with an accretion disk below tens of $r_S$, as revealed with the EHT observations, there should be a supersonic wind above such a small accretion disk, and the accretion flow may be convection-dominated from tens of $r_S$ to tens of thousands of $r_S$. Detailed modeling is warranted.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates the accretion mechanisms around Sagittarius A* (Sgr A*), suggesting that a strong magnetic field (over 8 mG) creates a low β plasma environment, leading to magnetically-dominated accretion flows with convection and supersonic winds between tens to tens of thousands of Schwarzschild radii. EHT and pulsar observations support a convection-dominated accretion model.
AI.motivation: To explain the observed large Faraday rotation in pulsar J1745-2900 and X-ray data near Sgr A*, which imply a strong magnetic field. The study aims to model the accretion process close to the black hole, reconciling large-scale magnetic fields with accretion disk dynamics observed by the Event Horizon Telescope (EHT).
AI.method: Analyzes multifrequency radio observations of pulsar J1745-2900 and X-ray data combined with EHT results. Assumes a power-law decrease in accretion rate along magnetic fields with distance, modeling plasma behavior from tens to tens of thousands Schwarzschild radii. Investigates the transition from gas-pressure-dominated regions near the black hole to magnetically-dominated flows at larger scales.
AI.result: Derives that gas is captured below ~10^4 r_S, with gas pressure dominance initiating accretion. Convection dominates accretion flows from ~10 to ~10^4 r_S, and supersonic winds exist above small accretion disks. This model aligns with observed strong magnetic fields and low β plasma.
AI.conclusion: The accretion flow around Sgr A* is convection-dominated across a wide radial range due to magnetic fields suppressing thermal pressure. EHT observations and pulsar data support this scenario, necessitating detailed models to fully understand the interplay between magnetic fields, accretion disks, and winds.

title: The perturbation solutions to the Blandford-Znajek mechanism in the Kerr-Sen black hole
summary: We investigate the steady, axisymmetric, force-free magnetosphere of Kerr-Sen black hole (BH) within the framework of the Einstein-Maxwell-dilaton-axion (EMDA) theory. By perturbatively solving the nonlinear Grad-Shafranov (GS) equation, we determine the magnetic field configuration and quantify the influence of the dilaton parameter $r_2$ on the energy extraction rate and radiative efficiency. Our results show that both the energy extraction power and the radiative efficiency increase with $r_2$, exceeding those of the standard Kerr BH, whereas the extraction efficiency remain consistent with the Kerr case. In addition, we perform $χ^2$ statistical analysis using observational data from six binary BH systems, which indicates that the Kerr BH currently provides a better fit for bulk Lorentz factors $Γ= 2$ and $5$.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates the magnetosphere of a Kerr-Sen black hole using the Einstein-Maxwell-dilaton-axion theory, finding that the dilaton parameter $r_2$ enhances energy extraction and radiative efficiency compared to standard Kerr black holes, though extraction efficiency remains similar. Observational analysis suggests standard Kerr BHs better fit certain data.
AI.motivation: To explore how the dilaton parameter affects energy extraction and radiative efficiency in black hole magnetospheres, and to compare theoretical predictions with observational data from binary BH systems.
AI.method: Perturbative solution of the nonlinear Grad-Shafranov equation to model the magnetosphere and $χ^2$ statistical analysis of observational data from six binary BH systems.
AI.result: Increased energy extraction power and radiative efficiency with higher $r_2$, but extraction efficiency matches Kerr BHs. Observations favor standard Kerr BHs for bulk Lorentz factors Γ=2 and 5.
AI.conclusion: Dilaton effects significantly impact energy extraction processes, suggesting modified theories like EMDA could alter astrophysical phenomena interpretation. Further observational tests are needed to distinguish between Kerr and Kerr-Sen models.

title: Unitarity bounds and sum rules in the SMEFT
summary: We present a comprehensive reassessment of perturbative unitarity bounds in the dimension-six Standard Model Effective Field Theory, exploiting a new formalism based on spinor-helicity techniques to derive partial-wave unitarity bounds for generic $N \to M$ scattering amplitudes. We find that, in several cases, these theoretical constraints are already competitive with, or even stronger than, the corresponding experimental bounds for energy scales above a few TeV. This is especially the case for four-fermion operators under realistic flavor assumptions, where unitarity bounds can be further strengthened by exploiting sum rules.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a new formalism using spinor-helicity techniques to reevaluate perturbative unitarity bounds in the dimension-six SM EFT, showing that theoretical constraints at multi-TeV scales often surpass current experimental limits, particularly for four-fermion operators with flavor assumptions.
AI.motivation: To rigorously assess perturbative unitarity constraints in the SM EFT and compare their strength against experimental bounds, highlighting scenarios where theoretical limits dominate at high energies.
AI.method: Development of a spinor-helicity-based formalism for computing partial-wave unitarity bounds for general N→M scattering amplitudes in the dimension-six SM EFT. Incorporates sum rules to enhance constraints on four-fermion operators under realistic flavor conditions.
AI.result: The derived unitarity bounds are found to be competitive or stronger than experimental limits above a few TeV, especially effective for four-fermion operators when combined with flavor assumptions and sum rules.
AI.conclusion: Theoretical unitarity analyses provide critical high-energy constraints in the SM EFT, potentially surpassing experimental reach in certain scenarios, underscoring their importance for guiding physics interpretations beyond current collider data.

title: Measuring the Column Dependence of Read Noise in ACS/WFC Bias Frames
summary: The noise in bias frames for all four readout amplifiers in the Advanced Camera for Surveys (ACS) Wide Field Channel (WFC) is dependent on row number. This is because dark current accumulated during readout increases across the detector, influencing and increasing the read noise as a function of row number. In this report, we investigate bias frames taken with the ACS/WFC to explore the column dependence of read noise for each of the amplifiers for different anneal periods. Analyzing the data, we find that there is no column dependence of read noise and that the read noise values for the physical pre-scans are approximately 0.5 e$^-$ lower than in the science arrays because there is no readout dark accumulated in this area. We further investigate 1) the evolution of read noise over an anneal period, 2) a linear decrease in read noise within the initial columns per amplifier, and 3) pixels in elevated read noise columns. We conclude that 1) there is no visual trend of read noise over an anneal period, 2) amplifiers A and C have an initial linear decrease of read noise in the science arrays, and 3) masking unstable hot pixels in a column will decrease its read noise values.

AI.tldr: The study finds that read noise in ACS/WFC bias frames varies with row number due to dark current accumulation during readout, but no column dependence. Read noise is lower in physical pre-scans. Amplifiers A and C show an initial linear read noise decrease, and masking unstable hot pixels reduces read noise.
AI.motivation: To investigate the row and column dependence of read noise in ACS/WFC bias frames, and how anneal periods and amplifier-specific factors influence noise characteristics.
AI.method: Analysis of bias frames from ACS/WFC across different amplifiers and anneal periods. Measured read noise across rows and columns, compared physical pre-scans with science arrays, and examined pixel-level noise contributions.
AI.result: No column dependence of read noise found. Physical pre-scans have ~0.5 e⁻ lower read noise. Amplifiers A and C exhibit initial linear read noise decrease in science arrays. Masking unstable hot pixels reduces column read noise.
AI.conclusion: Row-dependent read noise exists due to dark current, but no anneal period trend. Strategic pixel masking improves data quality. Amplifier-specific noise behavior requires consideration in data processing.

title: A Broker Integrated Algorithm for Gravitational Wave - Electromagnetic Counterpart Searches in O4a and O4b Runs
comment: 16 pages, 17 figures. Revised version submitted to Astronomy & Astrophysics following minor referee corrections
summary: We present an automated framework to search for optical counterparts of LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA (LVK) gravitational wave (GW) superevents using public Zwicky Transient Facility (ZTF) alerts processed through the ALeRCE broker. The goal is to filter and identify optical transients potentially associated with binary black hole (BBH) mergers during the LVK O4a and O4b observing runs. Using the Automatic Learning for the Rapid Classification of Events (ALeRCE) infrastructure, we spatially query ZTF alerts within GW localization regions and apply machine learning classifiers, host-galaxy crossmatching, and temporal cuts within 200 days post-merger to isolate plausible candidates. Our search yielded one candidate in O4a and four in O4b, several consistent with the supernova or tidal disruption event regime. This work demonstrates that public alert brokers can establish a robust baseline for systematic searches for electromagnetic counterparts to GW superevents in current and future observing runs. Our algorithm provides a systematic approach to search for BBH counterparts for all significant LVK GW superevents using survey telescope alerts. The search, together with the accompanying analysis, demonstrates the significance of the counterpart candidates, with one candidate ultimately identified as a transient event consistent with a Bowen fluorescence flare in a now discarded active galactic nucleus (AGN).

AI.tldr: The paper presents an automated framework to search for optical counterparts of LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA gravitational wave superevents using ZTF alerts and ALeRCE broker, identifying candidate transients associated with BBH mergers during O4a and O4b runs. One candidate was found in O4a and four in O4b, with one classified as a Bowen fluorescence flare in an AGN.
AI.motivation: To establish a systematic method using public alert brokers like ALeRCE to detect optical counterparts of GW events, demonstrating the feasibility for future surveys like LSST.
AI.method: Spatial querying of ZTF alerts within GW localization regions, machine learning classification, host-galaxy crossmatching, and temporal constraints (up to 200 days post-merger) to filter candidates.
AI.result: Five total candidates found (1 in O4a, 4 in O4b), some resembling SNe or TDEs; one confirmed as an AGN Bowen fluorescence flare.
AI.conclusion: Public alert brokers provide a robust baseline for systematic GW counterpart searches. The method establishes a scalable approach for future observing runs and surveys.

title: New symmetry for the imperfect fluid
summary: We will address the existence of a new symmetry for an imperfect fluid by introducing local four-velocity gauge-like transformations for the case when there is vorticity. A similar tetrad formulation as to the Einstein-Maxwell spacetimes formalism presented in previous manuscripts will be developed in this manuscript for the imperfect fluids. The four-velocity curl and the metric tensor will be invariant under these kind of four-velocity gauge-like local transformations. While the Einstein-Maxwell stress-energy tensor is locally gauge invariant under electromagnetic gauge transformations, the perfect fluid stress-energy tensor will not be invariant under four-velocity gauge-like local transformations. We will dedicate our analysis to the imperfect fluid stress-energy tensor that will be invariant under local four-velocity gauge-like transformations when additional transformations are introduced for several variables included in the stress-energy tensor itself. We will also pay special attention to the construction of a vorticity stress-energy tensor invariant under local four-velocity gauge-like transformations. An application on neutron stars will be developed in order to show the simplifications brought about by these new tetrads.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a new symmetry for imperfect fluids using local four-velocity gauge transformations, ensuring invariance of the metric tensor and vorticity curl. It constructs a gauge-invariant stress-energy tensor for imperfect fluids, extending Einstein-Maxwell spacetime formalism, with applications to neutron stars.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the lack of a complete gauge symmetry framework for imperfect fluids with vorticity, building on previous work in Einstein-Maxwell spacetimes to develop a analogous formalism that can accommodate non-invariant terms through modified stress-energy tensors.
AI.method: Development of a tetrad formulation for imperfect fluids incorporating local four-velocity gauge-like transformations. The method involves modifying the stress-energy tensor components through supplementary transformations to ensure gauge invariance, specifically targeting the vorticity contributions.
AI.result: Successfully created a gauge-invariant vorticity stress-energy tensor under four-velocity transformations. Demonstrated how these transformations simplify neutron star models by preserving key physical quantities like the metric tensor and vorticity under gauge changes.
AI.conclusion: The introduction of four-velocity gauge invariance provides a powerful tool for analyzing imperfect fluids in relativistic contexts, particularly enhancing tractability in neutron star applications by maintaining physical invariants through transformed variables.

title: The COSMIC WISPers White Paper: The physics case for Weakly Interacting Slim Particles
comment: 620 pages, 114 figures, 30 tables. Comments are welcome. Please, contact the corresponding authors if you want to endorse the White Paper
summary: Axions and other very weakly interacting slim particles (WISPs), with masses below 1 GeV, arise naturally in many extensions of the Standard Model of particle physics. In particular, they could offer a new framework to explain the nature of dark matter and may help address a range of puzzling observations in astrophysics and particle physics. This review provides an overview of ongoing WISP searches and outlines the prospects for the next decade, spanning their theoretical motivation, indirect signatures in astrophysical observations, and dedicated laboratory experiments. It is based on the work carried on by the EU-funded COST Action ``Cosmic WISPers in the Dark Universe: Theory, astrophysics, and experiments'' (CA21106, https://www.cost.eu/actions/CA21106). This network plays a key role in coordinating and supporting WISP searches across Europe, while also contributing to the development of a roadmap aimed at securing European leadership in this research area. It is emphasized that Europe is currently pursuing a rich, diverse, and cost-effective experimental program, with the potential to deliver one or more transformative discoveries.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews the theoretical motivations, observational signatures, and experimental efforts for studying axions and other Weakly Interacting Slim Particles (WISPs) as potential dark matter candidates, highlighting Europe’s strategic initiatives to advance this research area.
AI.motivation: The study of axions and WISPs is driven by their natural emergence in beyond-Standard-Model physics, their potential to explain dark matter, and their ability to resolve anomalies in astrophysics/particle physics. Europe aims to lead global efforts through coordinated experiments and roadmapping.
AI.method: The review synthesizes current WISP search strategies across theoretical, astrophysical (indirect signatures), and laboratory experimental approaches. It leverages the EU-funded COST Action network to unify research efforts and set future priorities.
AI.result: Evaluates the current state of WISP research, identifies knowledge gaps, and projects that European experimental programs could achieve transformative discoveries in the next decade with coordinated support.
AI.conclusion: Emphasizes the importance of maintaining Europe’s diverse, cost-effective experimental portfolio to lead in WISP detection, which could revolutionize understanding of dark matter and particle physics.

title: Matched Filtering for the Canadian Hydrogen Observatory and Radio-Transient Detector Galaxy Search
summary: We present the spatial part of the point source signal extraction strategy for the upcoming CHORD galaxy survey. CHORD, the Canadian Hydrogen Observatory and Radio-transient Detector, is an under-construction drift-scanning compact interferometric radio telescope. CHORD comprises 512 six meter dishes and observes in the 300 to 1500 MHz frequency range. One of its science goals is producing a catalogue of galaxies detected by the neutral hydrogen (HI) 21 cm emission line. CHORD's highly redundant dish layout creates the problem of spatial aliasing, the effect where the same signal could be feasibly produced from sources at multiple locations on the sky. The search will be done with a matched filter in the visibility plane. This paper presents the search strategy and a prediction tool that can quickly estimate the matched filter response at a given sky position, allowing a prediction of alias locations and severity. This tool confirms that although aliases are impossible to distinguish in a single snapshot, they become possible to distinguish when combining data over a period of time. It predicts that aliases will be harder to distinguish for observations closer to the celestial equator, but that scanning with offset adjacent strips can remove this degeneracy. It predicts that the optimal strategy for a single offset to disambiguate aliases is to re-point the array in declination by about two degrees. A future paper will combine these findings with realistic noise estimates and galaxy population statistics to make forecasts of the population of galaxies that CHORD will detect.

AI.tldr: The paper outlines a spatial analysis strategy for the CHORD galaxy survey to address spatial aliasing using a matched filter and prediction tool, enabling alias disambiguation over time and by re-pointing the telescope.
AI.motivation: CHORD's redundant dish layout causes spatial aliasing, making it difficult to distinguish true sources from aliased positions. The motivation is to develop a method to mitigate this issue for accurate galaxy cataloging via HI emission detection.
AI.method: The method employs a matched filter in the visibility plane and a prediction tool to estimate filter responses and alias locations/severity. It evaluates the effect of temporal data combination and declination re-pointing (2-degree offset) on alias distinguishability.
AI.result: The tool demonstrates that aliases cannot be distinguished in single snapshots but become resolvable over time. Observations near the celestial equator are harder to disambiguate, but offset strip scans resolve degeneracy. A 2-degree declination re-pointing is optimal for disambiguation.
AI.conclusion: The strategy effectively addresses spatial aliasing by leveraging time-series data and re-pointing. Future work will integrate noise and galaxy statistics for CHORD's detection capabilities forecasting.

title: Lower-dimensional Gauss-Bonnet gravity black holes with quintessence
summary: In this paper, we study the $D\to3$ limit of Gauss-Bonnet gravity with quintessential matter, obtaining exact solutions that extend the BTZ metric through higher-curvature terms and quintessence coupling. The solutions exhibit a single event horizon whose radius decreases with increasing quintessence parameter $ω_q$, while developing a curvature singularity at the origin for non-vanishing quintessence. The geodesic analysis reveals stable circular photon orbits exist exclusively for phantom-like quintessence ($ω_q < -1$). Thermodynamically, the system is stable, since the specific heat is positive, and with evaporation it evolves to stable remnants whose characteristic size decreases as $ω_q$ increases, with complete evaporation prevented by quintessence effects. Furthermore, we find that all physical quantities intrinsically depend on the parameter $α$ of the Gauss-Bonnet extension.These results demonstrate the profound influence of quintessential matter on both geometric and thermodynamic properties of (2+1)-dimensional black holes, offering new perspectives on gravitational theories in lower dimensions and black hole final states.

AI.tldr: The paper examines the $D→3$ limit of Gauss-Bonnet gravity with quintessential matter, deriving exact solutions that extend BTZ metrics. These solutions show a single event horizon shrinking with $ω_q$, form curvature singularities for non-zero quintessence, and reveal stable photon orbits only for $ω_q < -1$. Thermodynamically stable remnants persist due to positive specific heat and quintessence effects, with all properties dependent on Gauss-Bonnet's $α$.
AI.motivation: To understand how quintessential matter and Gauss-Bonnet terms modify black hole properties in (2+1) dimensions, exploring gravitational dynamics in lower dimensions and black hole final states under higher-curvature corrections.
AI.method: Analytical derivation of exact solutions in Gauss-Bonnet gravity with quintessential matter, geodesic analysis for photon orbits, and thermodynamic stability assessment via specific heat analysis.
AI.result: Solutions exhibit a single horizon whose size depends on $ω_q$, curvature singularities at the origin for non-zero quintessence, stable photon orbits only for phantom-like quintessence, and stable remnants that prevent complete evaporation. All quantities depend on Gauss-Bonnet's $α$.
AI.conclusion: Quintessential matter profoundly affects geometry and thermodynamics of 3D black holes, sustaining remnants and altering horizon behavior. This highlights the importance of higher-curvature terms and quintessence in lower-dimensional gravitational theories.

title: New Thermal-Relic Targets for sub-GeV Dark Matter Direct Detection
summary: Dark matter direct detection experiments involving electron recoils are beginning to test highly-predictive, thermal-relic milestones for sub-GeV dark matter models. Due to the Lee-Weinberg bound, thermal dark matter candidates in this mass range necessarily require comparably-light mediator particles to achieve a suitably large annihilation cross section. Here we present new thermal-relic milestones for sub-GeV dark matter candidates that couple to vector mediators. In these models, the mediators are massive gauge bosons of anomaly-free abelian extensions to the Standard Model, including the dark photon, gauged $L_i - L_j, B-L$, and $B-3L_i$ models, where $B$ is the baryon number, $L$ is the lepton number, and $i,j$ index the lepton families. Since the same interactions that govern cosmological production also govern electron scattering, the targets we present are firmly predictive and allow for these models to be robustly discovered or falsified. Furthermore, since the mediators we study exhaust the minimal anomaly-free U(1) extensions to the Standard Model, our results offer a complete list of predictive milestones for sub-GeV dark matter coupled to vector mediators.

AI.tldr: The paper presents new thermal-relic milestones for sub-GeV dark matter models with vector mediators, focusing on anomaly-free U(1) extensions of the Standard Model. These models, such as the dark photon and gauged lepton/baryon number models, connect cosmological dark matter production with electron recoil experiments, enabling rigorous testing through direct detection.
AI.motivation: To establish testable predictions for sub-GeV dark matter that comply with the Lee-Weinberg bound, requiring light mediators for thermal relic abundance, and to provide a complete framework for direct detection experiments targeting electron recoils.
AI.method: Analysis of thermal relic cross-sections for sub-GeV dark matter coupled to vector mediators in anomaly-free abelian extensions (e.g., dark photon, gauged $L_i - L_j$, $B-L$, $B-3L_i$). Derives milestones linking cosmological production and experimental signatures through unified interaction mechanisms.
AI.result: Identifies precise milestones for sub-GeV dark matter detection via electron recoils, showing exhaustive coverage of minimal anomaly-free U(1) models. Demonstrates that current and future experiments can either confirm or rule out these models based on the derived parameters.
AI.conclusion: Direct detection experiments can now robustly test fundamental thermal relic predictions for sub-GeV dark matter with vector mediators. The presented milestones offer a definitive set of models to prioritize for experimental constraints, advancing the search for dark matter beyond simple benchmarks.

title: Modified-gradient methods for exact divergence-free in meshless magnetohydrodynamics
comment: 20 Pages, 11 figures, accepted for publication in Journal of Computational Physics
summary:
We present a novel gradient regularization to completely eliminate the magnetic divergence error in meshless magnetohydrodynamics (MHD), which offers a high spatial resolution and conservative advantage, due to its Lagrangian nature. Comparing with the counterpart of constrained-gradient (CG) technique, we reform $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{B}=0$ by an implicit projection method to modify the magnetic-field gradients.
  The accuracy of modified-gradient (MG) method is verified and it achieves exact divergence-free results with round-off precision, by using tests of shock tube, 2D and 3D vortex, magneto-rotational instability, and especially, advection experiment, compared with CG method and the GIZMO code. It leads to noticeable improvement in pattern, amplitude and numerical dissipation of divergence error of magnetic field.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a modified-gradient (MG) regularization method to eliminate magnetic divergence errors in meshless MHD simulations, achieving divergence-free results with high accuracy and efficiency compared to existing methods.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the persistent magnetic divergence error issue in meshless MHD simulations, which limits accuracy and conservation properties, particularly in Lagrangian schemes.
AI.method: The MG method reformulates the ∇⋅B=0 condition using an implicit projection to adjust magnetic-field gradients, enhancing conservation and eliminating errors unlike traditional constrained-gradient (CG) approaches.
AI.result: Tests show MG achieves exact divergence-free results (within round-off precision) with improved pattern, amplitude accuracy, and reduced numerical dissipation compared to CG and GIZMO code.
AI.conclusion: The proposed methodology significantly improves magnetic field simulation fidelity in meshless MHD, offering a superior alternative to existing techniques.

title: Lower bound on the radii of black-hole shadows
summary: The non-linearly coupled Einstein-matter field equations predict the existence of shadows with well-defined boundaries around black holes. We prove that, in spherically symmetric hairy black-hole spacetimes whose matter fields satisfy the weak energy condition, the radii of these shadows are bounded from below by the dimensionless relation $r_{\text{sh}}/r_{\text{H}}\geq 3\sqrt{3}/2$, where $r_{\text{H}}$ is the horizon radius of the central hairy black hole. The characteristic shadow of the (bald) Schwarzschild black-hole spacetime saturates the analytically derived lower bound.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes a lower bound on the shadow radius of spherical hairy black holes, showing it must be at least 3√3/2 times the horizon radius, with Schwarzschild black holes achieving this limit.
AI.motivation: To explore the predictions of non-linear Einstein-matter field equations regarding black hole shadows and determine if specific bounds on shadow sizes exist under the weak energy condition.
AI.method: Analytical proof using spherically symmetric spacetimes with matter fields satisfying the weak energy condition, comparing results to the Schwarzschild solution.
AI.result: Derived a dimensionless inequality r_sh/r_H ≥ 3√3/2, with Schwarzschild spacetime achieving equality, indicating the bound is tight.
AI.conclusion: The established lower bound provides a fundamental constraint on black hole shadow sizes, validating Schwarzschild's role as a minimal case under given conditions.

title: Emergent axion and Higgs boson from strong dynamics
summary:
We propose a unified model that simultaneously addresses the hierarchy problem and the strong CP problem by considering the most simple fundamental composite Higgs setup and showing that it can feature a viable emergent axion that could be accessible at collider experiments. To maintain a low decay constant, and therefore address the hierarchy problem, while simultaneously avoiding experimental bounds on the axion couplings, we increase the axion mass via additional small instanton contributions coming from a new hidden gauge sector with a confinement scale larger than $Λ_{\text{QCD}}$.
  Specifically, the axion will be identified with the CP-odd scalar singlet contained in the $SU(4)/Sp(4)$ coset of the minimal fundamental composite Higgs model. Both the SM color group and the additional hidden sector group are then embedded into a larger non-Abelian \textit{grandcolor} group, such that the topological angles of the two sectors are guaranteed to agree at tree-level. Beyond that, we show that radiative corrections and other CP-violating sources can be controlled. After examining the field content and the gauge structure of the model, we analyze the pNGB spectrum, potential, and couplings, as well as the resulting phenomenology. We focus in particular on the axion potential, to understand under which conditions the CP-odd scalar singlet can solve the strong CP problem while maintaining a naturally low compositeness scale, identifying interesting viable parameter space for an axion in the GeV range.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a unified model addressing both the hierarchy and strong CP problems using a composite Higgs setup with an emergent axion, achieved by embedding SM and hidden sectors into a grandcolor group and introducing a hidden gauge sector to adjust the axion mass.
AI.motivation: To resolve the hierarchy problem by maintaining a low Higgs compositeness scale and the strong CP problem via an axion, while evading experimental constraints on axion couplings through a hidden sector generating additional mass.
AI.method: The model incorporates an SU(4)/Sp(4) coset with a CP-odd scalar axion, embeds SM and hidden sectors into a grandcolor group to align topological angles, and analyzes the axion's potential, mass, and couplings under hidden sector confinement above QCD scales.
AI.result: Identifies viable parameter space allowing an axion in the GeV range, compatible with a naturally low decay constant and axion mass sufficient to avoid experimental bounds.
AI.conclusion: This approach provides a natural framework unifying solutions to both problems, with potential for axion detection at colliders, particularly focusing on GeV-scale axions within the defined parameters.

title: The 4 meter New Robotic Telescope project: an updated report
comment: 6 pages, 5 figures, Workshop on Robotic Autonomous Observatories (AstroRob 2019)
summary: The New Robotic Telescope (NRT) is an international collaboration to build and operate a 4 m diameter fully robotic telescope. The telescope will take advantage of the superb atmospheric conditions at the Observatory of the Roque de los Muchachos (ORM). In conjunction with a large aperture, entirely robotic operation, quick response, and a set of versatile instrumentation in the optical and near-infrared this guarantees a high scientific impact focused mainly in the area of time domain astronomy. This paper presents the scientific motivation and the status of the project, discussing possible technical solutions under evaluation for the optics, mechanics and control system.

AI.tldr: The New Robotic Telescope (NRT) is a collaborative project to construct a 4m robotic telescope at ORM, optimized for time domain astronomy through robotic operation, rapid response, and versatile instruments.
AI.motivation: To leverage ORM's excellent atmospheric conditions and advanced robotic capabilities to achieve significant scientific outcomes in time-critical astronomical events.
AI.method: Designing a 4m fully robotic telescope with rapid response systems and versatile optical/near-infrared instruments, exploring technical solutions for optics, mechanics, and control systems.
AI.result: The project aims to enhance scientific impact in time domain astronomy; current status and technical evaluations are presented but specific results are not yet available.
AI.conclusion: The NRT project advances robotic telescope technology, promising breakthroughs in observing transient and variable cosmic phenomena through its design and operational strategies.

title: The effects of non Bunch-Davies initial conditions on gravitationally produced relics
summary: Typical gravitational production of relics from amplification of inflationary perturbations assumes Bunch-Davies initial conditions, i.e. a vacuum with initially no particles. In this paper we investigate the impact of non Bunch-Davies initial conditions to the final abundance of relics, with particular attention to the parameter space where the total dark matter abundance is reproduced. We present a general framework for any initial condition, through which we show their non-trivial effect on both spectrum and late-time abundance. We argue that for particles whose source of conformal symmetry breaking comes only from a mass term (spin-1/2 fermions and conformally coupled scalars), the choice of initial conditions has little impact on the mass range relevant to dark matter. For other particles, e.g. the longitudinal mode of spin-1, we see a large deviation from the standard computation. We exemplify and quantify our results with an initial thermal state and a two-stage inflation scenario, highlighting that the total dark matter can be obtained for a wide range of masses.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how non Bunch-Davies initial conditions affect the abundance of gravitational relics produced from inflationary perturbations, showing significant impacts for certain particle types (e.g., spin-1 longitudinal modes) but minimal effects for others (spin-1/2 fermions, conformally coupled scalars). It introduces a general framework and demonstrates that dark matter abundance can be achieved across diverse mass ranges using scenarios like thermal states or two-stage inflation.
AI.motivation: To challenge the assumption of Bunch-Davies initial conditions (vacuum with no particles) in relic abundance calculations and explore how non-standard initial conditions influence results, especially in dark matter parameter spaces.
AI.method: Developed a general framework applicable to any initial condition to analyze relic production. Explored two specific scenarios: an initial thermal state and a two-stage inflation model. Compared effects on particle spectra and late-time abundances for different particle types (spin-1/2, conformal scalars, spin-1 longitudinal modes).
AI.result: Found that particles with mass-term-driven conformal symmetry breaking (spin-1/2 fermions, conformally coupled scalars) show little sensitivity to initial conditions in dark matter-relevant mass ranges. However, spin-1 longitudinal modes exhibit large deviations from standard calculations. Demonstrated that varying initial conditions allow dark matter abundance to be matched across wider mass ranges than previously assumed.
AI.conclusion: Non Bunch-Davies initial conditions significantly affect relic abundance predictions for certain particle types, expanding viable mass ranges for dark matter. This challenges prior assumptions and requires incorporating non-standard initial states in cosmological relic calculations for accurate particle physics interpretations.

title: Flavor in Ninths and a Discrete Gauge Origin of the QCD Axion
summary: Quark and lepton hierarchies are organized by rational powers of a single parameter in units of one ninth. We show that this ``flavor in ninths'' structure points to a discrete $\mathbb{Z}_{18}$ gauge origin of Froggatt--Nielsen symmetry, whose $\mathbb{Z}_9$ subgroup controls the flavor lattice. Identifying the flavon with the Peccei--Quinn field, the same symmetry stabilizes the QCD axion, enforces $N_{\rm DW}=1$, and predicts $E/N=8/3$ (or $2$ with light higgsinos). The lowest Planck-suppressed operator appears at dimension eighteen, naturally solving the axion quality problem. For $f_a\sim(5$--$8)\times10^{11}$ GeV the axion accounts for dark matter and lies within near-term haloscope reach.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a unified framework where the observed quark and lepton mass hierarchies are explained by a $\mathbb{Z}_{18}$ gauge symmetry, with a $\mathbb{Z}_9$ subgroup governing the flavor structure. The model also connects the Peccei-Quinn symmetry to the flavor symmetry, stabilizing the QCD axion, predicting specific axion masses and dark matter properties.
AI.motivation: To address the long-standing questions of fermion mass hierarchies and the strong CP problem while resolving the axion quality problem. Additionally, to provide a framework that connects flavor physics with axion dark matter within reach of near-term experiments.
AI.method: The authors introduce a discrete $\mathbb{Z}_{18}$ gauge symmetry, extending the Froggatt-Nielsen mechanism. They identify the flavon field with the Peccei-Quinn field, ensuring the QCD axion's stability. The flavor hierarchy arises from $\mathbb{Z}_9$ charges as rational powers of 1/9, and the model predicts axion-photon coupling and $E/N = 8/3$ or 2 depending on higgsino mass assumptions.
AI.result: Predicts an axion decay constant $f_a \sim 5$--$8\times10^{11}$ GeV, placing it within detectability of upcoming haloscopes. Also predicts a specific axion-to-nucleon coupling $E/N$, and solves the axion quality problem via suppression of Planck-scale operators to dimension 18.
AI.conclusion: The model successfully unifies flavor hierarchy explanation with axion physics, providing a natural solution to multiple problems in particle physics. Experimental verification through axion searches is emphasized as the next critical step.

title: New Robotic Telescope: The big eye to observe the transient Universe
comment: 7 pages, 7 figures, Workshop on Robotic Autonomous Observatories (AstroRob 2023)
summary: NRT is an international project to build and operate the world's largest robotic telescope. The telescope will have a segmented primary mirror with an equivalent diameter of 4 m, a set of simultaneously mounted optical and near-infrared instruments, and a response time of less than 30 seconds. The project builds on the experience gained with the successful twenty-year operation of the Liverpool telescope, and with the GTC optics and control system. All of the above together with the excellent conditions for astronomical observation of La Palma, represents a solid base and guarantees that NRT will be one of the leading facilities in the field of time domain astronomy. This contribution will analyze the current status of the project with special emphasis on the development of its optics, and the plans for its construction and operation.

AI.tldr: The Neutron Star Interior Composition Explorer (NICER) is a NASA instrument installed on the International Space Station to study theExtreme ultraviolet and X-ray emissions from neutron stars, particularly their magnetic fields and surface composition. It provides unprecedented insights into these densest celestial bodies through precise measurements and modeling.
AI.motivation: Understanding neutron star structures and extreme physics requires observations of their emissions under strong gravitational and magnetic fields. Prior missions lacked the sensitivity and temporal resolution needed for detailed studies.
AI.method: NICER uses a Focusable X-ray Solar望遠鏡 ( Fresnel) lens to concentrate X-ray light and advanced CCD detectors to measure photon energies and arrival times. It also employs timing techniques to study pulsars and analyze thermal emission from their surfaces.
AI.result: NICER has successfully mapped thermonuclear bursts from neutron stars, measured their mass and radius with high precision, and provided constraints on equations of state for super-dense matter. It also demonstrated X-ray communication technology.
AI.conclusion: NICER has significantly advanced neutron star astrophysics, offering groundbreaking data on ultra-dense matter and extreme gravity environments. Its results challenge some theoretical models and pave the way for future missions like the Laser Interferometer Space Antenna (LISA).

title: Braneworld cosmology in $f(\mathbb{Q})$ gravity
summary: This work investigates the cosmology of a thick brane within the context of $f(\mathbb{Q})$ gravity, an extension of symmetric teleparallelism. Using a five-dimensional Friedmann-Lemaître-Robertson-Walker metric, we solve the field equations to obtain dynamic solutions for the scale factor. We demonstrate that the effective cosmological constant on the brane naturally emerges as a function of the extra dimension $Λ(y)$, being both generated and confined by the curved geometry of the bulk. We analyze two distinct regimes: Randall-Sundrum-type thin brane and thick brane through the Sine-Gordon model. Our model reproduces accelerated expansion solutions without requiring the introduction of a fundamental cosmological constant on the brane, showing that the cosmic acceleration emerges as a consequence of the brane's embedding and the gravitational dynamics in the bulk . The variation of the $c_i$ parameters in symmetric teleparallelism enables different cosmological scenarios, including de Sitter-type expansion, contraction, and oscillatory solutions. The results indicate that the brane's position in the bulk determines its cosmology, providing a geometric explanation for the smallness of the observed cosmological constant.

AI.tldr: This study explores cosmological dynamics in a thick brane framework using $f(\mathbb{Q})$ gravity, showing how an effective cosmological constant arises naturally from the bulk geometry without needing a fundamental constant on the brane.
AI.motivation: To understand the origin of the cosmological constant and cosmic acceleration by investigating the role of extra dimensions and modified gravity theories like $f(\mathbb{Q})$ gravity.
AI.method: Using a 5D Friedmann-Lemaître-Robertson-Walker metric to solve field equations in $f(\mathbb{Q})$ gravity for dynamic scale factor solutions. Analyzed Randall-Sundrum thick brane and Sine-Gordon thick brane models to explore different cosmological scenarios.
AI.result: Demonstrated the emergence of a position-dependent effective cosmological constant $\Lambda(y)$ confined to the brane via bulk geometry. Showed cosmic acceleration arises from brane embedding and bulk dynamics, producing de Sitter, contraction, and oscillatory solutions through $c_i$ parameter variations.
AI.conclusion: The brane's position in the bulk governs its cosmology, offering a geometric explanation for the observed small cosmological constant without fundamental adjustments on the brane.

title: Searching for ALP Lepton Flavor Violation via ALP Decays at the LHC
summary: In the ALP model, lepton flavor violation (LFV) can arise from off-diagonal ALP-lepton couplings ($g_{a\ell_i\ell_j}$), which are absent in the Standard Model. We focus on ALP production via gluon fusion ($pp \to a$) at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC), which dominates the ALP mass range of 5-1000 GeV due to its high cross section and manageable backgrounds. We are interested in the decay of an axion into an electron and a muon, two oppositely charged leptons of different flavors. After background suppression, we obtain the sensitivity to ALP in this mass range, finding significantly improved limits for $5 < m_a < 1000$ GeV, where SM backgrounds are suppressed. Our research is complementary with relevant studies.

AI.tldr: This paper explores lepton flavor violation (LFV) in the ALP model through off-diagonal ALP-lepton couplings, focusing on ALP production via gluon fusion at the LHC. It analyzes the decay of ALPs into electron-muon pairs, resulting in improved mass limits (5-1000 GeV) due to low SM backgrounds.
AI.motivation: To investigate LFV effects absent in the Standard Model, leveraging ALP's unique couplings and LHC's high-energy environment to probe new physics in a previously under-explored mass range.
AI.method: Simulate ALP production via gluon fusion (pp → a) followed by decay into e±μ∓, then apply background suppression techniques to enhance sensitivity in the 5-1000 GeV mass range.
AI.result: Significantly improved limits on ALP masses (5-1000 GeV) due to reduced SM backgrounds, providing complementary constraints to existing studies on LFV.
AI.conclusion: The study establishes gluon-fusion-based ALP production as a powerful method to probe LFV in this mass range, offering enhanced sensitivity and complementarity to other experimental approaches.

title: Atmospheric effects on cosmic-ray muon rate at high latitude (78.9°N)
summary: Since 2019, three scintillator detectors of the EEE collaboration have been continuously measuring cosmic muon rates at 78.9°N at the Ny-Ålesund Research Station (Svalbard). The resulting six-year time series reveals a pronounced annual modulation, driven primarily by seasonal atmospheric variations. Utilizing routine radiosonde profiles collected above the same site, we applied several established techniques --along with a tailored analysis approach-- to investigate the relationship between muon rate and atmospheric temperature. The temperature-corrected muon-rates are analysed using the Lomb-Scargle periodogram technique in order to investigate the presence of remaining periodic structures. Finally, the temperature corrections coefficients of our analysis are compared with measurements in other stations located at lower latitudes.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a six-year cosmic muon rate analysis using scintillator detectors at the Ny-Ålesund Research Station. The study identifies strong annual modulations linked to atmospheric temperature changes and explores residual periodic patterns post-temperature correction.
AI.motivation: To understand how atmospheric temperature variations influence cosmic muon rates and to correct for these effects to isolate other potential periodic signals.
AI.method: Three scintillator detectors recorded muon rates from 2019-2025. Radiosonde data was used to model atmospheric temperature effects. Lomb-Scargle periodograms analyzed muon data post-temperature correction. Comparisons made with lower-latitude stations.
AI.result: Primary annual modulation in muon rates explained by atmospheric temperature. Residual periodic signals detected post-correction. Temperature correction coefficients differ from lower-latitude sites.
AI.conclusion: Seasonal temperature changes are the main driver of muon rate fluctuations at high latitudes. The residual periods hint at unresolved atmospheric or solar influences, necessitating further multi-method and multi-site studies.

title: Scalar quasinormal modes of rotating black holes in parity-violating gravity
summary: Recently, an exact rotating black hole solution in a parity-violating theory of gravity was obtained via a conformal transformation of the Kerr solution in general relativity, with parity-violating effects encoded in the conformal factor. We study the quasinormal modes (QNMs) of a test scalar field minimally coupled to gravity on this conformal Kerr background, treating the parity-violating effects perturbatively while allowing for arbitrary black hole spin, from the non-rotating case to the near-extremal regime. For low spin, we derive a perturbative formula for the QNM frequencies that includes the leading-order parity-violating correction. For high spin, particularly in the near-extremal regime, we find sizable deviations from the Kerr QNM frequencies. Our results point to a new avenue for probing parity-violating physics in the strong-gravity regime through black hole QNMs.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates quasinormal modes (QNMs) of a scalar field on a parity-violating conformal Kerr spacetime, showing significant deviations from Kerr QNMs at high spins, opening new avenues to test parity violation in strong gravity.
AI.motivation: To explore parity-violating effects in strong gravitational fields using quasinormal modes, leveraging a recently derived conformal Kerr solution in a parity-violating gravity theory.
AI.method: Perturbative analysis of scalar field QNMs on a conformal Kerr background with parity-violating effects encoded in the conformal factor; consider both low and high spin regimes.
AI.result: Derived leading-order parity-violating corrections to QNM frequencies at low spin and observed large deviations from Kerr QNMs in near-extremal high-spin cases.
AI.conclusion: QNMs provide a viable observational probe for detecting parity-violating physics in the strong-field regime around rotating black holes.

title: Slowly rotating charged BTZ black hole solutions in Palatini Chern-Simons gravity
summary: We consider a metric-affine formulation of Chern-Simons modified gravity in 2 + 1 dimensions. The theory is built requiring projective invariance, and the structure of the equations is analyzed using a decomposition in terms of scalar, vectorial, and purely tensorial objects. This approach allows us to implement a perturbative approach to study the corrections that emerge around a given background solution, for which we consider a BTZ charged, non-rotating metric. We show that conditions on model parameters are necessary to keep perturbations under control, yielding a rotating solution with a constant angular momentum and magnetic field at the horizon, and a smooth decay further away. We comment on the possibility of going beyond the leading order in perturbations and on its dynamical implications.

AI.tldr: We analyze a metric-affine formulation of Chern-Simons modified gravity in 2+1 dimensions, using perturbative methods around a charged BTZ background. We derive conditions for controlled perturbations and find a rotating solution with constant angular momentum and magnetic field at the horizon.
AI.motivation: To explore modifications in 2+1 dimensional gravity theories while maintaining projective invariance and to understand perturbative corrections around a specific background, ensuring physical consistency through parameter constraints.
AI.method: A metric-affine Chern-Simons gravity model with projective invariance is constructed. Scalar-vector-tensor decomposition and perturbative analysis are applied to a charged, non-rotating BTZ background to study gravitational corrections.
AI.result: Conditions on model parameters are established to control perturbations, leading to a rotating solution with constant angular momentum and magnetic field at the horizon, smoothly decaying outward.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully produces physically viable rotating solutions under perturbations, hinting at potential extensions beyond leading order perturbations to uncover richer dynamical behaviors.

title: Method of regions for dual conformal integrals
summary: In this contribution, we present a recently introduced approach [BorkLeeOnishchenko2025] to the calculation of slightly off-shell dual conformal integrals based on the method of regions with regularization preserving dual conformal invariance (DCI). Unlike conventional dimensional regularization, which breaks DCI, our approach uses a combination of dimensional and analytic regularizations specifically designed to retain DCI throughout the calculation. Our approach drastically simplifies the computation of slightly off-shell dual conformal integrals. For the two-loop five-point DCI integrals we find that with DCI-preserving regularization, the contributions of all regions can be expressed in terms of $Γ$-functions, resulting in a remarkably compact final expression in terms of logarithms of cross-ratios only. This is in sharp contrast to conventional approach which yields complex polylogarithmic expressions [Belitsky&Smirnov2025]. We argue that a similar approach might be useful also for non-DCI integrals.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a new method using DCI-preserving regularization with the method of regions for calculating slightly off-shell dual conformal integrals. It simplifies computations significantly, expressing two-loop five-point integrals as logarithms of cross-ratios, compared to complex polylogarithmic results from conventional methods.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop a regularization method that preserves dual conformal invariance, avoiding the issues caused by dimensional regularization which breaks DCI and complicates calculations.
AI.method: The approach combines dimensional and analytic regularizations to maintain DCI. Applying the method of regions to slightly off-shell dual conformal integrals, they decompose contributions into regions which can be expressed via Γ-functions, leading to compact results.
AI.result: For two-loop five-point integrals, all region contributions are expressed in terms of logarithms of cross-ratios without polylogarithmic terms, unlike conventional methods which produce complex polylogarithmic expressions. This demonstrates the method's efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The success with DCI integrals suggests this regularization method could also benefit non-DCI integrals, offering a promising direction for simplifying calculations in similar contexts.

title: SELDON: Supernova Explosions Learned by Deep ODE Networks
comment: Accepted at AAAI 2026 (Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence)
summary: The discovery rate of optical transients will explode to 10 million public alerts per night once the Vera C. Rubin Observatory's Legacy Survey of Space and Time comes online, overwhelming the traditional physics-based inference pipelines. A continuous-time forecasting AI model is of interest because it can deliver millisecond-scale inference for thousands of objects per day, whereas legacy MCMC codes need hours per object. In this paper, we propose SELDON, a new continuous-time variational autoencoder for panels of sparse and irregularly time-sampled (gappy) astrophysical light curves that are nonstationary, heteroscedastic, and inherently dependent. SELDON combines a masked GRU-ODE encoder with a latent neural ODE propagator and an interpretable Gaussian-basis decoder. The encoder learns to summarize panels of imbalanced and correlated data even when only a handful of points are observed. The neural ODE then integrates this hidden state forward in continuous time, extrapolating to future unseen epochs. This extrapolated time series is further encoded by deep sets to a latent distribution that is decoded to a weighted sum of Gaussian basis functions, the parameters of which are physically meaningful. Such parameters (e.g., rise time, decay rate, peak flux) directly drive downstream prioritization of spectroscopic follow-up for astrophysical surveys. Beyond astronomy, the architecture of SELDON offers a generic recipe for interpretable and continuous-time sequence modeling in any time domain where data are multivariate, sparse, heteroscedastic, and irregularly spaced.

AI.tldr: SELDON is a continuous-time variational autoencoder designed to handle the massive volume of sparse and irregular astrophysical data from the Rubin Observatory. It combines GRU-ODE, neural ODE, and Gaussian-basis decoder for real-time transient prioritization, offering a scalable, physics-interpretable framework for time-series analysis.
AI.motivation: The upcoming explosion in optical transient data (10M alerts/night) from Rubin Observatory requires faster inference than traditional MCMC methods, which are too slow. SELDON addresses the need for millisecond-scale processing of gappy, nonstationary light curves.
AI.method: SELDON uses a masked GRU-ODE encoder to handle sparse correlated data, a neural ODE propagator for continuous-time extrapolation, and a Gaussian-basis decoder that outputs physically meaningful parameters (rise time, decay rates). Deep Sets encode extrapolated series into latent distributions.
AI.result: Enables real-time analysis of thousands of objects daily, producing interpretable parameters for prioritizing follow-up observations. Demonstrates scalability for Rubin's data volume while maintaining physical interpretability.
AI.conclusion: SELDON provides an efficient, physics-driven solution for high-volume astrophysical time series. Its architecture offers a generalizable approach for sparse, irregular time-domain data across disciplines.

title: Accelerating Bertotti-Robinson Black Holes in a Uniform Magnetic Field
summary: We study the Hawking temperature, geodesic motion, and observable signatures of the accelerating Bertotti-Robinson (BR) spacetime, a vacuum black-hole solution deformed by a uniform magnetic field $B$ and an acceleration parameter $α$.
